You're going to learn very quickly that when I say, I think out loud, I really think out loud. So some of my answers are going to be circuitous. And I hope. jumping the questions, which is really what I want to focus. So the first thing I want to talk about is that there is no right way. In the martial arts, we have a metaphor that there are many paths up the mountain. If you talk to someone who does Brazilian jujitsu, or take a window, or Kempo, or something, there's all this, what's the best martial art? And the metaphor is everybody's walking up the same mountain as you get farther and farther up the mountain. You get closer and closer, sort of in principle, to each other. That's how I feel about paid traffic, and who says what in the business. I hear this all the time, where someone says, well, I took so-and-so's course, and he said, or she said, to do X. What do you think? I think at a certain level, everybody is right, and all of us are wrong. So it's context-dependent. Anything that's credible doesn't always work. So I'm giving you one person's perspective. I don't want to suggest in any way that this is the right way. This is a way that I think it's principles-based. It leverages what André has created with sphere of influence. And it's a way to do things that I think will work better, and in my experience, do work better in this modern take on marketing, which is getting less and less tolerant of nonsense. And I will have a rant later about that. So what's most important about which way works is which way works for you. And you have to test and find out if straight lead gen just absolutely wrecks it for your business. And you have a money printing machine because of it, and someone's telling you to do something different. Be smart. Do the thing that works. Try something different. See if it works better. But don't ever assume that what I'm saying today is this is the only way. This is a set of ways. Second thing that I think is probably the most important lesson I've learned in my 47 years on this earth, which is avoiding big mistakes is a lot better for long term performance than trying to be innovative continuously. This is something that Warren Buffett talks about a lot. His business partner, Charlie Munger, talks about it. You don't have to be innovative. You just have to not avoid big mistakes. It's that business advice that the number one way to make money is stop losing money. A specific example for ad words that I see all the time, this is an enormous mistake, is not using negative keywords properly. So someone sets up an ad words account and they don't use negatives. I just ran into this example last week where someone, it's not a client of someone who I was giving a proposal to. I looked at their account and they do water damage remediation. So if you have a flood in your home, linky sealing, whatever it is, they come in bidding on terms for water damage. Well, the most popular terms and the most expensive, like $25 a click plus, are for iPhone and iPad water damage. They don't do that. They clean your carpets. They do whatever. But they were paying a nice chunk of change for traffic that they had no hope to ever convert. So that's the kind of mistake you can't outrun. It's like that saying you can't out exercise a donut. Andre has his CrossFit shirt on. So if Andre goes and does CrossFit six days a week and finishes his CrossFit session with a donut and a couple slices of pizza and a 35, 40 ounce, I guess you probably would be leaders for you, Andre. A couple of leaders of Coke. His CrossFit's not going to help. You can have the best CrossFit workout in the world, but it's not going to outpace the mistakes he's making with nutrition. That's the idea. Facebook, a classic mistake in Facebook is ignoring the impact of account equity or giving that equity to somebody else, or an agency will come in and say, oh, we'll manage it all for you. Don't worry about it. And then they build the custom audiences and on and on and on. And you don't have that equity. I see that happen less than it used to. There used to be this terrible fear of accounts getting banned. Had clients come to me and said so and so, says that they have a whitelisted account. It's all nonsense. There's no such thing. There never was any such thing. But it's that kind of idea that, well, I'll just let you do it and build all the equity in your account, because I'm scared of what might happen if I try it. That's a typical mistake. So if you avoid those mistakes, then you're not trying to carry an anchor around with you all the time, because it's just too tiring to do that. It's too impactful negatively on a business to make mistakes continuously. I'm not that smart to outpace them. Next item, framework before work. You can't see my whiteboard really behind me. That's the thing that I leave on my whiteboard. It's the only thing that never gets erased. Framework before work. Spir of influence is a framework-informed strategy. This particular framework and strategy, Andres, developed is based on principles and fundamental principles, which I think is where it gets its value. It's not going to be different 10 years from now, which is rare in our world. But when you are thinking about tactics, tactics are informed by strategy. And that strategy is informed by this overall framework. And it's framework first. And I think that's incredibly important. I want to do an Andre Chabron thing right here and open up a loop about how it's strategy is the second most important thing. So yeah, it was like, what's the first? But I'm just going to tell you, so it's not really an open loop. But I did my best, Andre. So this is my, I hope, one of my few rants today. But here's my big soapbox rant. Value first, marketing second. And I think in terms of values, who do you serve and how do you contribute positively to their lives? I love to use the value proposition canvas for this. If you go to the Strategizer website, S-T-E-R-A-T-E-G-Y-Z-E-R, there are tons of free tools. You can learn everything you need to know. You don't need the $5.50 or $5.95 value is in all their free tools. You don't need to spend $800 or $500, whatever it is, the training. There's so much value in the free account. And it will walk you through a process where you identify a market's jobs to be done, the pains associated with doing those jobs, the gains the audience hopes to create for a specific audience segment. And then you can map your products, gain creators and pain relievers. And you prioritize and you match up the highest priority one. You can do this in an hour and a half. I do this all the time. I teach entrepreneurship at a local college. It's the most impactful thing I teach my students. And it is all about value first. It's not your funnel quotes. It's not this interesting, curious, entertaining expression of your big idea. It's not some new hacker trick that somebody's selling. It's certainly not that. It is value first. The biggest mistake I see, and I see this a lot, and it's the most impactful mistake I've seen in 25 years of being sort of doing what I do now, is having good marketing and service of a bad business. I don't mean bad in the judgmental sense, but a business that just isn't really delivering value, that gets back to this can out exercise, a donut idea, that at some point, you get caught in this or it catches up with you. So, does that mean that we should all just give up marketing? Absolutely not. Marketing is a good marketing is a great tool for a good business, a great tool for amazing business, but it's not something that's going to fix a bad business. So if you're not quite sure how your business delivers value to somebody and who you're delivering it to, I would start by answering that question very clearly first. I'm not saying anything you haven't already heard of people like Seth Godin who made a career out of this one principle. Go listen to them way more than me, but I mention this because there's no trickery. There's no way to, the phrase that I think I heard growing up is you can't put lipstick on a pig, so don't try. Next item on my list, I'm going faster than I thought, so I'm pretty excited. I might get to 15 minutes to get through all this and then really hit the questions. So the other concern that we have is survivorship bias. So it's this idea that, you see this all the time, top 10 tips to be successful like Richard Branson or like Warren Buffett or whatever. And then some genius will say, well, even better, let's look at Richard Branson and Warren Buffett and Steve Jobs and Elon Musk and let's figure out the 10 things that they have in common. And then those are what you should do to be successful broadly defined. Here's the problem, for every Elon Musk and every Warren Buffett and every Steve Jobs, there are 10,000, 100,000 people who did pretty much the same thing and failed miserably. So survivorship bias, if I on this call today tell you about my top 50 greatest hits in digital advertising, digital marketing, I'm going to leave out the top 500 that failed and often those 500, very similar to the 50 that worked. The variables that made the difference were not in what I did, it was sort of outside what I did. And that's just reality. So what I hope when we talk today, when I answer questions that I want to avoid survivorship bias and say, here's, if I can, if I have real examples, here's where the answer that I'm giving worked, this is where a situation where this was the result. And here's a very similar situation where it did not work. So what do we think the difference is? What do I think the difference is? Try to do that as much as possible. It's very easy to look at what worked and pull lessons from that. And sometimes those lessons are accurate, but a lot of times survivorship bias makes them less accurate and less useful because there's nothing more irritating than doing exactly what somebody did who succeeded in failing miserably and not knowing why, not realizing that you're in good company, that most people who did the same things probably failed at it too. There is, what is most impactful, I think, for survivorship bias and thinking about survivorship bias is that you need to get to a meaningful result for your idea and your business as quickly as possible to see if what you're doing is working specifically for you, all variables considered. I had two clients at one time who were friends in a very similar industry, not really directly competing, because I have a rule where I don't work with competing companies as an agency, but they were both, they're both friends, they had been business partners, so they knew that I was working with them individually. That strategy was almost identical. It was a 100% sphere of influence, influenced strategy, where there was content on the front end that was attractive to the subset of their audience, that the critical, responsive subset of their audience, it was well-written, it was beliefs-based, we ushered people through it. One of them produced outstanding results, results measured in eventual revenue, so leads were generated. This person had a okay email process to get to the product. Product pricing was very similar, did very well, other person failed spectacularly, did not make a single sale after 90 days. Same strategies, same industries, same approach. What made the difference? Who knows? There were factors, and I don't wanna get into the specifics of these two clients because I wanna always maintain confidentiality, but the differences were not so obvious that we could look at the end and say, well, this is why, or that's why. I share this because sometimes things don't work, and we don't know why, and it's getting in and testing for your business and latching onto the things that work as fast as you can, and discarding the things that don't work as fast as you can, I think it's the path to greatness. Two things left here quickly, this is a weird one, but I changed my mind about something recently, and I wanna share that because it's enough of a change in my thinking that it is kind of permeating out, and I think it'll show up in the call today, so without me giving background, you think, wait a minute, that seems very strange for him to say. Not that I won't say strange stuff in this entire call, I will, I just wanna eliminate this one. I have said for a long time that one of the benefits of what we do is that we have decimal point accuracy in our tracking, which is theoretically true, and I have any tool that exists for tracking the most robust tools imaginable, I've used them, and I get it, I know the value propositions, I know how they work, I've used them for clients, sometimes for years, sometimes for decades, however, as I observe my own buying behavior, my own interaction with advertising behavior, I realize that being, you just had to have my phone only rings when I'm on a call, so I had to, there. It's the watch out. Yeah, it's funny, I unplugged it too, so to avoid that. As I've observed my own behavior, as I, when I see an ad, or engage with content in some capacity, and then later make a buying decision, I've just very recently started to sketch that out, like what happened? And there are very significant holes in any tracking's capability to report against what I did, in many cases. Many cases, if you could see the whole picture, you would have an idea of what I did, and you might be able to intuit from the data, cause and effect, but I'm less convinced that we do, that we have true cause and effect tracking in place, and the thought that we do makes us make decisions that are not always the best decision. So there are things that we need to track and really care about. I think first page, the second page conversion in a multi-page presale site is the most impactful thing to track initially, because it just tells us, have we opened up something of interest to someone enough to get them to that next page? Like to me, that is the indicator of quality in the beginning. I'm not suggesting that that marker is not accurate, but what I do think is less accurate is the global picture of what actually contributed to somebody who had no awareness becoming aware, and then from that awareness engaged in some capacity to become a lead or something else, and then eventually converted to become a customer. I think we are less able to track that with a cause and effect relationship than we believe. I think we've diluted ourselves a little bit. In aggregate, our tracking is probably very accurate, but at the individual level, I think the paths are too, there are too many different paths. So the net effect for this, I think, is that adding value and being focused on the customer and really how you're appearing in the world, I think has more impact than tracking would suggest, that I've just seen too many cases in how I've behaved and how others behave where an initial interaction with a minor interaction with somebody set off a chain of events that were just not trackable that later led to a positive opinion of a seller or a person or something that you just couldn't see what happened in between because so much of it happens in between here, and that's sort of mystery land. So anyway, I didn't mean that to turn into a rant, but I think it'll show up in some answers to questions today. I wanted to have the background why. Last thing I want to mention, I hinted at this earlier, this work is hard, and when I say this work, I mean, writing the content, developing the ideas, getting into the paid advertising, creating the ads, strategizing, this is all very, very hard, and everyone sells easy, and easy's bullshit, plain and simple, it's not easy. And what I don't like about selling easy is it sets up this very demoralizing expectation. It's like, I built this funnel and it makes a good jillion dollars a day, and all you need to do is build a funnel just like it, nonsense, that's total nonsense. Or I had this one campaign and now I'm driving Lamborghini, and it's complete nonsense, maybe they are driving Lamborghini, whatever, but most likely that what you're seeing as an end product is not nearly as impressive as it seems, sort of, as it's presented, but it's also not showing the blood, sweat, tears, fear, toil, anger, you name it, that went into making that success, and when you're sold easy and you run into hard, you start to think that the problem is you. You're not the problem, this is hard work. So if we go into it, knowing it's hard, expecting it to be hard, when it's hard, okay, we're prepared, but what I've seen happen a lot, and I've worked with a lot of students over the years, where the moment they run into difficulty, they think, well, something must be wrong with me, maybe I need to know more, maybe, no, nothing's wrong with any of you, nothing's wrong with us. This work we've chosen is hard in getting harder, and there's no way around that. So when you run into it and realize, well, I just did this test and it failed, right, welcome to my world, 12 hours a day, we do tests that fail, we have brilliant ideas that fail spectacularly, we have ideas that are mediocre, that just are incredible, that's the reality. So with all of that as background, I can close this document, I can jump into questions. Andre, anything you wanna jump in with an initial question to get started, how do you wanna handle questions? Yeah, I mean, that was, I think people are definitely gonna re-listen to that little rant that you've just done, you've just gone through a load of really cool things. And there's a lot of positives to it's hard and it's getting harder, and one of them is that it's hard for everybody, right? So people that are coming into just our spaces and they are trying to do what we're doing or trying to copy each other, it's really hard for them and they're gonna fall off. So if you know that it's hard and you stick in there and you figure it out, you're gonna win and they're gonna lose. You know, this is not necessarily a zero sum game sort of mindset, but you know, it is hard and it's good to have that mindset that this isn't gonna be easy. True. Yeah, and value first, that's the cornerstone of everything that we do. And when you don't do that, it's not a long-term strategy. And in many cases, you can, Harpen-Hüpler's gonna out-convert value first on the front end. So if you're tracking that, sometimes it's gonna seem that, well, if I harped up slightly or make these big promises, oh look, it's converting better, but that's not a long-term vision and that's certainly not a long-term strategy because the other things can win out every day when you really back it out and you look at a wider timeframe. That's where it's gonna win and tracking's never gonna show that. Right. And that's a better expression of what I meant about tracking too is our time horizons with tracking are wonky. We might see like, oh, in 21 days, these eight things happen and we got a result. But in nine months, that person has come back and done something that doesn't really make sense, cause and effect, but it's because of the positioning and the value that was added and the feeling like this person is really, when I need to be involved in that world, whatever it is, if it's buying a product or consulting or whatever it is, like I'm gonna go back to that person. I may not need it now, but when I do, that person's on my shortlist and I'm gonna go back to that company and gave me a good experience in the past. And that's the kind of stuff that's really difficult to track, but it's the difference between a healthy business and a struggling business. Absolutely. So, I can see there's a bunch of people putting questions in the checkbox, which keeps scrolling, so it's difficult to keep track of them. Yeah, and then when needed, kind of share my screen out if we need to see some imagery of some of the pictures. Put some of that psychedelic background on. We can really be entertained. All right, so Luke asked, what is account equity? Great question. So, account equity in, particularly in Facebook, but also anything that has retargeting capabilities, custom audience capabilities, so analytics has account equity as well because you can build lists. Over time, I'll use Facebook as an example because it's just easier. Over time, whenever there are actions taken in Facebook, that action has a certain value that you as the advertiser own. So, it's just for the sake of argument, say that you put together a single ad that goes to a long form copy that's across five pages, multi-page presale site, and you are optimizing for, you're asking Facebook to optimize for people who see the second page. That's your point of conversion. So, instead of opting in and saying, the thank you page is the trigger for a conversion, the point of conversion is that they've seen page two. So, we run that, and once Facebook hits 25 conversions, they start, then their targeting gets a little bit better, their algorithm gets a little more powerful. When you get up to 75 to 100, then they're really working on your behalf. They're using their big data, their algorithm to optimize for that result. So, what has happened is, you now have this small cohort of people who have taken the action that you want. There's equity in that because that audience, as it grows, you can go to Facebook and say, I would like to build a custom audience based on people just like these people who did what I wanted them to do, which was convert to page two. So, and then Facebook will come back and say, here are a trillion people who match that. And say, well, I just want the top 1%. It's called a top 1% look-alike audience. So, I want this audience that's most like these people. I wouldn't do it with 75, and I might do 100, 200, 500, whatever. But now you have a point of equity because now you've asked, you've structured something so that you've got a result and you've built on the success of that result to get a better result because now you have another audience to go back to that's supposed to be like the best part of the audience you originally targeted. And then that happens across the way. You could say, all right, well, how about people make it to page three? And usually what happens with a multi-page presale site format is you have equity across four different places. So, first one, I'm assuming long form copy, not video. I mean, you could do a video version of the multi-page presale site. So, first equity is in conversion from first to second page. Now you have an audience of people who have done that. So, you get a refinement. Then you have an audience of people who have opted in. That's even better because they've made it all the way through your entire multi-page presale site and they've taken the action that you want, which is they've become a lead. Well, now you have an audience to build for people who are like that. And then you can go back and start engaging with that audience, hoping that you've increased your chances to get those conversions because the audience that you're targeting is most like those people. And then eventually you make a sale and that sale generates a point of conversion and you're starting all over, you're saying, well, in an ideal world, I don't care so much about who became a lead, who saw my second pay, or whatever, what I care about is who made it all the way through and bought. Show me those people. Now you have, and this creates equity. And you own that equity. My complaint, and what I saw a lot in the past is the agency or the consultant would sell this idea that you don't want your Facebook account banned. So we'll just use mine. And then I build all the equity and I build all the lists of responders and the audiences, whatever else. And then I do affiliate sales based on what everybody else paid for by paying. But I don't like how that feels. That doesn't happen as much anymore, but I do hear it out there. I also hear this idea about white listed Facebook accounts. I can tell you categorically, that has never been true. So if anyone has ever told you that they have a white listed Facebook account, that they will advertise for you on your behalf, that is 100% false. They may have a squeaky clean Facebook account, they may, but Facebook does not white list accounts. When my company is a VIP managed Facebook agency, I've had this conversation because I've lost, I've lost many opportunities over the years because people have said, well, this company says they have white listed accounts and you don't, there's no such thing. So rant over. Build the account equity yourself in your account and then build on that equity. And then when you build on the equity, you get compounding effects because the equity becomes a feedback loop, that you have a better audience to target initially and then you get a better response and when you get a better response from a better audience, you get a new list of potential people to build a new list and even better audience that you can go back to. And it's this like infinitely, it's not recursive, it's whatever the opposite of recursive. So, I don't know, it's a good one to look up later. Let's see. Cool. Okay, are you gonna go in order or are you just gonna pick one? I was trying to go in order, but somehow I saw one and then I just didn't, I thought I was answering the first one and then I didn't see. So, I've answered the first one, so I've moved it out. Oh, you're sorry, see, this is really learning stuff. How do I define value for an affiliate program? It's a good question. So, there's sort of, I guess there's two parts. What is the value that the affiliate offer provides and to whom does that provide value? So, what's the value proposition for the person making the sale or the person providing the product? That's the first part. So, if I sell red widgets and you're an affiliate of mine, there's a value that I provide a certain customer through the ownership of the red widget. Your value as an affiliate could be as simple as making your audience aware that red widgets are available from someone who provides high quality red widgets or you could do a value add, you could do something like, say, if it's a learning program, you might offer a bonus where you take, okay, I've gone through the program myself and I've put together, I think, Andre, you did this with something with a mind map, maybe it was E5 or something where you create like another layer of value. Say, I've gone through this program and here are the highlights and here's, I'll give you this thing when you buy it for me because I believe this amplifies the value of the original thing and there are lots of ways to do that. Sometimes it's a, if it's a product, it's something to add with the product. If it's a course or other things, then that is either complimentary or sometimes it's just bringing it to somebody's attention. You forget that there is a currency. And I'm kind of stealing one of Rich's, Rich Sheffrin's ideas here because he uses the phrase that secrets are a form of social currency. So I want, he's responsible for this idea when I think about it. Referring people to products or courses or something cool, that is social currency. I love doing that. If I see something interesting, I can't wait to tell the people I think it's most relevant to in my circle of friends and others. So that could be just awareness. If you have a, say you have a list in your list, maybe isn't aware of, you know what's going on in the marketplace and you make them aware of it and you offer a product, there's value in that. It can be abused certainly, but I do believe there's a lot of value in that depending on how you do it. All right. Yeah, I think value is, you know, it's a mindset and it's universal in the sense that if you are thinking in a way that you're always thinking about delivering value, whether it's your own product or if it's an affiliate offer, you know, it's that mindset that's on that top layer because then you're always going to be picking to try and figure out how can I add something to this? You know, if I was this person, if I was the customer, the consumer, you know, what would be valuable to me? And I think that's a mindset that you see to think of and then it's almost become irrelevant whether it's an affiliate offer or your own offer because it's going to be the same deal, right? Right. There's also something, you know, there's a transfer of enthusiasm that I think is valuable to like, you know, there are certain products that I, that have really affected me. Arm is one of them, Honor Responder Madness is one of them, certainly Sphere of Influence is one of them. Wouldn't be on the call if it weren't. There are others who go through an exhaustive list. I've been doing this forever, but there are some that stand out. And for me, it's less, it's not sort of the affiliate thing, but it is a transfer of enthusiasm to say, you know, I know what you're, you know, I think about this like the airplane seat thing. Like if I say something sits next to me on an airplane and they tell me something and I am familiar with it and I know that, you know, and maybe I did three or four different courses in that world and one was just head and shoulders above the rest. To go to that person and say, oh, I absolutely totally get this. I did this and I did this and I did this, but wow, this one was absolutely incredible. This is where I would start. That transfer of enthusiasm is in itself valuable. Now, can you be enthusiastic about every product that you refer to an affiliate relationship, your choice? You know, I can't make that choice for you, but it's certainly a lot more fun to be wildly enthusiastic about it. All right. Yeah, totally. I think it's important to, that's an important point. And, you know, sometimes it's difficult to think in a certain way if there's no revenue tied directly to that decision. And so for example, because one of the articles I'm writing that's going to go out at some points is on security for, you know, for people like us and using VPN. Well, the top VPN, in my opinion, is the one that I use. There's no affiliate program, right? But there's no affiliate program for the second best one. Well, I'm not going to refer people to the second best one just because there's an affiliate program and I can use the money from it. I'm going to refer them to the one that doesn't have an affiliate program because it's the best one, in my opinion, and it's what I use. Yeah, and that's, you know, you don't have to make money on every interaction too, is a great thing. Like, you know, and there doesn't have to be any trickery like this is, you know, here's the one I use. There's another, you know, there are others. I use this one, period. I mean, that's it. And some, you know, there might be something else like they're 50 available. I think these are the top three. I get paid if I, you know, any one of these three is purchased as an affiliate. Okay, I mean, it's just that blend. I mean, not to have to have change of that model. The people notice, right? Because then they go to the service and then they can refer to their friends for they check to see if there's an affiliate program and there's none that says, oh, well, Andre referred me to this thing, even though there was absolutely no money to be made inside it. And then that equity will come back to you down the line, you know, when you're releasing a product or whatever. So you can't measure that. Yeah, that's the stuff we can't measure in six months, but that was impactful that somebody remembered. We can't measure it yet till you get electrodes in your head, Andre. We call them our heads. Okay, next question. Do you ever partner your online campaigns with offline materials? Yes. Many of our clients are in very traditional industries, resorts, heavy equipment manufacturers, shipping, you know, geotechnical equipment around the world and they have offline, they have online. You know, how becomes the question? There are some sophisticated tracking tools. There's certainly 800 number tracking that's very impressive and certainly, and you can 50 layers deep, you can go with that. There are also URL tracking. We probably have done more URL tracking for clients in the past than anything else. We've done, one comes to mind where they had, they spent a tremendous amount of money for gift certificates at the Christmas holiday and they advertised on radio, local newspaper, larger newspaper, TV, and then they had something on the homepage of their website and then we did paid search and a couple of other things. And we just, anything that was offline, we used a different URL or we would use subdomain or something and was it 100% accurate? Absolutely not. It was directionally right in an aggregate it was right and what it showed this particular client, this was many years ago, what it showed this particular client was that the local newspaper outperformed all of the other newspapers together and that there was one radio station that had 10 times of performance of everything else combined. So next year when it was time, except for their homepage there, but that's sort of weirdly recursive. So the next year when they went to figure out what they were going to pay for, for advertising is very clear. Eliminate all of these, pay for these, spend 15% of what they used to spend and get 85% of the result. It was great. So how to do that gets complicated, especially when there's, if you have marketing materials that are handed out that are printed and other things, it's difficult, but I think we can't be blind to this idea that we only advertise on digital media and that's it. It really depends on the context of your business. All right. Bill asks, how in depth is your thought on survivorship bias? It strikes me that this is essential to understand that it isn't always how to. It's connecting on a deeper emotion. So my depth of understanding about survivorship bias is probably mildly informed novice. Like it's something that strikes me as being wildly obvious now that I'm aware of it. I've had other experiences in the past where I think somebody was telling me something that later I realized was a survivorship bias thing. So, but I think this is something that I've become more interested in recently, partly because I'm just so sick of seeing all the how-to nonsense that's available and having probably tried every how-to, how to get better at X idea imaginable and not necessarily succeeded. And some things that are very near and dear to me like this idea of deliberate practice and K. Anders Erickson's work, now I'm starting to look back and wonder like, okay, well, is there survivorship bias? And I mean, to the people who had the very specific type of practice and the commitment to the practice and other things, were there other people who did the exact same thing and didn't succeed? I mean, I don't know the answer to that. So I think survivorship bias is very important for us to be aware of just as human beings, certainly to be aware of as professionals who are doing marketing work because anytime we see the case study that I did X and Y happened or the five of my clients went through my program and they got these results, okay, excellent. But let's assume that you had a thousand clients and how many of those thousand did exactly what the five did and didn't get those results? Like that's the kind of question that it's on my mind. I don't, it can be wildly debilitating and also an excellent form of procrastination. And I love to intellectualize to create procrastination. I have a PhD in it where like, oh wait, this is fascinating. Let me tug on this thread for a few hours. The queen of true but useless is something that I have to be aware of all the time. Like is this true? Yes, can I act on it in a way that's impactful for me? Not necessarily. You know, I do think there's value in saying, here are some things that show up consistently for people. You know, meditation's a great one. If anyone's ever been, you know, listened to 10 or 15 of Tim Ferriss's podcast, there's this weird thread that seems to run through many high performers, they meditate, okay. There are a lot of people who meditate who are not high performers too. So it's just that, again, I'm procrastinating with intellectualizing so I'll stop doing it. So the short answer, that was the long answer or short question, the short answer, the short question is, I am an informed novice trying to be more aware of the impact of survivorship bias in my thinking. Let's see. How do you keep your motivation going when you go through a bunch of failures? That's such a good question. So two ways, I'm paid to stay motivated. I mean, that's a good one. If I don't stay motivated, I don't eat and I like poverty a lot less than I like demotivation, so I mean, that's not really the question you're asking, you know, the question is sort of that broader question, which is, if we were to switch roles, I mean, you have a failure, have a failure, have a failure, you know, how do you keep going? And that's a brilliant question. I think first of all, we have to pull failure as an emotional term out of our vocabulary. Failure is still an economic term and economics do you have emotions associated with them? So if you spend $500 in ads, spend in 10 hours of your time on something that does not produce the result that you wanted it to produce, you did spend $500. So I don't wanna suggest we just pretend like, hey, it's sunshine in unicorns, just go do another one. $500 is $500, $50 is $50. So there is an emotional component of that. I think going in with the expectation that you are fact-finding more than in researching, more than your quote unquote advertising, that helps, it helps me. I like to come in and I like to have a hypothesis, and the hypothesis may be, you know, an entire narrative arc for a campaign or it may be a hulk to get into a campaign or it may be that problem or solution or story is the best approach for a market. I try to distance myself from the result and just, and I believe in beginner's mind too, and it's a hard concept to really internalize. I spent a long time trying to internalize it, meaning that I don't expect myself to have the answer. I don't expect myself to be right. I don't even expect myself to be right more than I'm wrong. A bunch of you are now like, why are you even on this call? That's what I mean by that is, there are too many variables for me to know in any industry, including my own, what's going to resonate with a subset of an audience at any given time with all of the things going on in their lives, news, you name it. So I try not to attach to the outcome itself. I attach to the overall outcome that everything that I do is going to move me closer to the outcome that I want. That maybe, I have an idea that I think is outstanding. I throw $100 at it and all I hear are crickets. It just didn't work. Okay, there's tremendous value when not wasting more time going down that road. And maybe I try something I'm not as excited about and that really works. Well, that's an opportunity for me to step back and say, why did that work versus that? The harder thing is the string of failure is phenomenon where 10 things didn't work or five things didn't work. And the money that was spent feels a lot more real. You know, real is different for everybody. It could be 100, it could be 10,000, whatever is real for you. That's different. And I try to do my best to not get to seven to 10 without really knowing what's going on. Like take a step back. Is this idea just not good? Is this product just not what the market wants? I will often, if I have four or five campaigns, a campaign, when I say campaign, I don't mean necessarily ad campaign campaigns or like a thought experiment, but I have four or five that aren't getting traction. I do go back to the value proposition canvas as a thinking tool and a Sharpie and Post-It's. And I look at it and I really ask myself, is this really a need? This thing that I'm trying to poke at, a gain or a pain, this thing that I think really matters, maybe it doesn't matter. Maybe it doesn't matter now or maybe how I've expressed it. But I want to go learn from that. I don't want to wake up in a year and say, wow, I'm Thomas Edison, I have a thousand ways not to create a light bulb. I don't want a thousand ways not to do something. I want four or five before I get something that tells me, oh, okay, there's a little something going on there. It's not a perfect answer, but it is how I think it's hard. So I jumped in on it, I jumped in front of you earlier. I was just going to say that I don't think failure is a binary thing. You know, it's not, you know, you may have failed, but you've spent some money to learn something, even if it's come back negative, because like you said, you then iterate from that point. That's a point that didn't exist before that failed campaign, right? So version two and version three, it's gonna be better. Like you said, you don't get to number seven and it's still not working because that's a signal, right? That's a signal that there's something bad, something's wrong. Yeah, and it's, you know, someone, I saw someone pop, you know, mentioned, you know, to replace excitement with, you know, it's very, I don't, I'm paraphrasing because it sort of kicked off something that I'm familiar with too. So I apologize if I grab somebody else's idea and then made up my own version of it. So, but the, my understanding from what I've read and in my own experience when this happens, the physiological feeling of excitement and anxiety are interchangeable. You don't really know the difference. We tell ourselves that there's a difference. So the butterflies in the stomach, the shortness of breath that's sort of high in the chest, the tightening of external muscle, all that stuff that's anxiety or concern is it's physiologically it's the same as excitement. So, yeah, the life hack version is, you know, just tell yourself, I'm excited. I don't know, I think there's an author who talks about this like, say I'm excited. Maybe it's Debbie Millman or something he talks about. You know, when you feel anxiety or anxious about, you thought it was the experiment was going to be this wild success and it wasn't, you just tell yourself like three times, I'm excited. I'm excited, I'm excited. Okay, maybe, I don't know. The way I try to make those two things interchangeable is just to recognize that my emotion is not the feeling, even though they happen so close in time, I think they're the same thing. And I just remind myself that the feeling can trigger either. I can, I have to decide. And this was probably the most profound realization I had many, many years ago. And I believe it was Steven Covey in seven habits of highly effective people, which I didn't, full disclosure, couldn't read. I couldn't get through the reading, but I listened to it with the CDs and it was excellent. But one of the things he talks about is that, what makes humans different from everybody else is there is a gap between stimulus and response. And that may be true for other creatures as well, but we know it's true for us that there's a stimulus. There's a physiological feeling, and then there's a response. The response is my choice. And there's a space in between when I get to make that. Sometimes we have to tease out the space. So if I'm, and this has happened, and it's, when I say, mentioned earlier, I get paid to be motivated, well, I also don't get paid when things go horribly wrong. So, for an extended period of time. And when things aren't working, I absolutely experience the anxiety that all of you would experience if you're working on your own or a client's campaigns. That, if I'm trying things and trying things and they're not working, and we're not getting the result that the client wants and the client's paying me for, I feel that at a visceral level. And it's not always easy to say, oh, this is exciting. It's not always very exciting. So where I've gone with that is, I try to get to next best action as quickly as I can, because I don't want to intellectually and emotionally stew on what does the failure mean? So I want to see that something happened, make a decision about what I think needs to happen next and get into the action associated with that decision as quickly as possible so that I don't start getting caught up in the feeling and the always-mean nonsense. I mean, once I'm acting, and once I'm onto the next thing, I instantly feel better, because now I have a sense of agency over the situation. It's a long, again, long answer to a question. Let's see. What do you think about the value of funnel hacking, the expectation that it will be easy? That's a good question. Actually, Andre, I'm going to give you that one. What do you think about the value of funnel hacking? I guess what's the definition of funnel hacking? I think there might be a few. I mean, my perception of what funnel hacking is is probably different to the official Russell Brunson version. So I don't know what your version is, but for me, it's just... Almost reversing engineering someone's funnel or their self-process. It's less about hacking your own funnel to try and make it slightly better. So I'm not quite sure which day he's talking about or what your definition of funnel hacking is. When I think about funnel hacking, I think about this idea that somebody out there has done the work to build out the entire funnel and that we can kind of see it. We can also deconstruct how they advertise it. We can deconstruct and imagine all these variables and then just replicate the whole thing, but our way and expect the same result, or an even better result, as we can leapfrog. In theory, I can see how that would work. In practice, I don't see it work very often. So there are so many, and I've been in countless meetings with particularly higher-level internet marketers who they're getting some data that would be visible to any of us, but then they're years, decades of experience, churns, effectively decision engines, and they say something that channeling all this experience into this one little change that they then run with and it produces a result. And that's hard to hack. Those that level of, how did they zig when I might have zagged? All that stuff is hard. I think there's a lot of things that are invisible. There's invisible in the sense that unless someone's reading every single word and understanding the context of every single sentence and the meaning of everything right on top of each other, or stacked back, and understanding that entire narrative, it's very difficult to really do that, to funnel hacking justice. So for me, it's more of a meta thing. Someone's doing this, they've got a front-end, they've got a triple-eye or whatever, and that's the basic process, the thing that they've built on. But all those little pieces in between, all the nuances, that's where the value is, or that's where some of the big leverage points are, and you can't always funnel hack that very easily unless you're really good at understanding context and really getting into the week. I think there's a framing thing that happens to you that nobody wants to talk about, and I'm not sure everybody's even aware of it. This is a top five way not to be popular with your internet marketing friends, if you're at a conference, but there is a, within particularly, in many industries, there is a scaffolding of authority that's been created that everybody else is, that the big players benefit from. So I had this happen to me at a conference last fall where I wasn't supposed to speak, there's a cancellation at the last minute, I was on a panelist of speakers, and I was introduced as an expert by someone who was running the event. So prior to that, almost no one in the room had any clue who I am or what I did. So I could have had a conversation with them, and it would have been entirely different. But the moment I was framed as the expert, then everything changed, and there were tons of conversations, and lots of stuff happened as a result of that, because of the way somebody else presented me and framed me. I think in particular, with affiliate things that are happening, and with email and other things, that may have a funnel that by itself, without any of that, sort of directed credibility from someone who says, I mean, no one's great, well, this isn't true, this is broadly thematically true. We're not necessarily great because we're great at something, we're generally great because other people say we're great at something. And that's an overstatement, but there's a great TED video about leadership, what is the critical factor in leadership? And if you've seen it, it's outstanding. There's somebody who is at an outdoor concert dancing wildly like a fool, and he's crazy right up until the point that he gets what's called the first follower. And the first follower is who gives the leader all of her initial credibility because you're not a leader without a follower. So, and then other people join in or whatever, and the person who started it is in the center of the action, and the point being made is leaders don't exist without followers, authorities don't exist without audiences. So, when someone has a funnel that's absolutely amazing and it's generating a quadrillion dollars every 15 minutes, and you can copy that too, sure, if you can also copy all of the directed credibility that the affiliates and others fire at that funnel too. So, that's kind of tertiary, but I see that happen a lot, nobody talks about it. And everyone's like, no, no, you can do this too, just all you need is the structure. Structure is not what is doing the work. All the factors that feed the structure is doing the work. So, I think I convinced myself, I don't think funnel hacking is very valuable. Oh, I might change my mind later though. So, I don't think funnel hacking is the panacea that it's been made out to be. I mean, sure, you need a mechanism to sell your stuff and to talk about your stuff, but just copying the way somebody else has done it, the same way they did it is actually the same. Long answer to a short question. All right, what is the biggest mistake people make when marketing intangible products? Well, that's, I've been ended. Yeah, that's rough. Intangible, so. Clean this on the chopper. There's value, right? That's like a major thing that's always gonna exist. So, that's a mistake. People don't think about that. So, the biggest mistakes, I don't know, it's so broad. There's a million things. I think a huge mistake is not connecting something to a felt need. That to me, if you have something that's not tangible, it'll still sell. Lord knows how many tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of dollars I've spent on intangible things over the years that are very valuable to me. It's training and education, on and on and on. They're not, it's not really tangible, but all of it connected to a visceral felt need that I have. Like a need to either develop a skill, to learn something that was interesting, on and on and on. So, I think in value proposition design is good for this. You know, Donald Miller's story brand kind of talks about this right at the beginning that we have an external need that is paired with an internal emotional representation. So, we have this external need to provide financially for our family, and there's an internal need about not being entirely sure if we're worthy or not kind of stuff. Like that's the kind of weird thing that, in his model of the customer's the hero when you are the guide. So, you're sort of meeting somebody who is saying, wow, I need to figure out a way to make a bunch of money. But what they're really struggling in is this very strong emotion. It's like, I'm not sure I can do this. I'm not worthy. It's the hero's journey. So, I think if we don't make that connection, or if we don't tap, and I don't love, I don't want to hammer on somebody, especially the negative stuff. I don't like that. That's just not how I'm wired. Even if I know somebody's really struggling with, you know, whatever a sense of not feeling worthy or whatever. I don't want to go pound on that and try to amplify it to then offer them a solution. That's just not me. I would rather meet somebody where he or she is and say, I get it. This is not fun. And here are some ways I think we could get you to a place that's better. Or you just have some head trash going on to borrow Perry Marshall's term. And, you know, let's look at the evidence objectively. You know, do you really feel like you're not, you know, if you look at the evidence, is this really helpful? I mean, that kind of thing. So, I think for anything that's not tangible, we have to make it tangible by connecting it to something that somebody feels very deeply. And in many markets, people have, especially the most responsive customers, have tried and failed a lot. And there is this emotional component of, am I just setting myself up for failure one more time? That, you know, I don't want to push that button, but I want to be aware that that is what's going on a lot of people's minds. And to connect to that and to be honest about it, I think is incredibly important to say, you know, the story ran that Donald Miller uses the Luke Skywalker Obi-Wan Kenobi thing. You know, the example of the hero versus the guide, you know. So, you didn't hear Obi-Wan Kenobi have a conversation with Luke Skywalker. We went through a laundry list of everything that was wrong with him and why, you know, he should feel terrible. It was the guide meets the hero where he or she is and finds what's good about them and figures out a way to build on it. That's really the hero's role or the guide's role. So, I like that better if you have an intangible thing that connects to a felt need that you can say to somebody, subtly, I get it, I know where you are. This is how this relates. I think something in there is the answer to that question. Yeah, totally. I think that's the biggest mistake I see people making is they don't understand the audience's needs deep enough. At best, they have a very shallow understanding of it. You know, my audience wants to do weights. That's not the right answer. All right, next one. This is the one I've been waiting for this whole time where it's like you said something here and then you contradicted yourself later. I'm like, oh, of course I did. Of course I did. All right, question is in principle, and one in your training you say, you can't convert the perfect audience. If you can't convert the perfect audience, then the problem is your offer. But then in principle three, you give an example of how a client paid for focus groups and defined their perfect audience, then that audience didn't buy. My question is, why did you switch the audience rather than try and change the offer for the perfect audience to get them to buy? So two part answer to this question, they paid focus groups to tell them to give them a perfect audience without actually defining the most important outcome. And they made a huge mistake in validation, which is if you have a product that you're going, if you're trying to, this is straight out of lean. So if you straight out of lean business, if you're trying to validate a product or validate a product idea, and there's going to be a fee associated with that product. Say it's $29 a month. And you have an opt-in waiting list form. And that's all somebody can do is opt in to be on the waiting list. And then you run 10,000 people to that and 5,000 of them sign up to be on the waiting list and you just declare, hooray, we've done it, we've validated idea, your hour idea. You haven't validated anything because you don't have a business till you have a paying customer. So validation in that example to find the perfect audience would require that, yes, they signed up to be on the wait list, but then you made an offer and said, we're going to, we have a founders discount, whatever it is, it will normally be $29 a month, but we want to do three months for $29. What's the take rate on that offer? Because if it's zero, you didn't validate your offer, you just validate, there are a lot of people who are interested in it for free. So this client thought they were validating the market because they went to the market with the idea. This is a physical product, but they went to the audience with an idea and the audience loved the idea, but they never gave them the opportunity to actually take money out of their pocket and pay for the idea. And even that wouldn't have been entirely accurate in a focus group setting because you're kind of on the spot. And this was a paid focus group setting. So someone says, okay, well, here's the product and it's $50 and you just made 250 and you're like, oh, is this part of the deal? So they could have wildly messed that up. What they thought they were doing was finding the perfect audience. They just had the wrong criteria because they didn't take it all the way to the conclusion as well this audience buy. So that's the first part of the answer is they didn't actually target the perfect audience based on what ultimately was going to make the business successful, which was sales. The second part of the question is, why did you switch the audience rather than try to change the offer? This is a very specific physical product. I can't name the product because it'll give away the company. So a very specific one that they've brought to market, they have since added additional products that are sort of complimentary products. So there was some of that thinking, but the real challenge for this market was that the market could satisfy itself without having to make a purchase. I use an example of Dave Asprey a lot. I don't know if it's a perfect example, but maybe it's at least useful, imperfect example. So Dave Asprey is a bulletproof coffee guy. If you want to be in his tribe, that the price of entry is, you can go consume his content, you can read his articles, you can do whatever, but ultimately, if you really want to be inside the tent, you need to start drinking bulletproof coffee. And that's kind of the entry point. Now, maybe you don't, whatever, drink the coffee for some reason and you buy some of his other stuff, but really to kind of be in that world, to fulfill the full circle, you need, in my opinion, most people need to buy something. You might eventually buy his coffee, you might buy the brain octane oil. I mean, certainly you can use your own coffee, you can buy butter, grass-fed butter at your local store, and you can buy some other MCT oil, but to really do it the way he's talking about, his super high quality products kind of fulfill it. So to scratch that itch, if you resonate with his message, you go, ah, I never realized this is great, I wanna do this, you kind of have to give him money to close that loop. With this other client of mine, you could, their focus group audience, could consume all their content, could feel good about it, could have the same political views, could do all of these things, and they didn't need to buy to be inside the tribe. And that's where I think the point was. I think Tim Ferriss is probably a decent example of this too. You know, 200 plus, I don't know what he's up to for podcasts. You don't need to buy his books to get value from him. I think most people do, I think they probably closed the loop, a lot of them were introduced to him by from four hour work week, but you could just go and spend, I don't know, the next two years listening to free podcasts, and sort of be in the tribe and comment, and do some life improvement stuff, and feel part of it without having to spend a dime. Now, you know, he makes affiliate, you know, things he's got, you're part of his advertising base, so sure, you're contributing some value, but you don't actually have to spend money. So I think one of the challenges with perfect audiences is, are my structured in a way that they need to buy to be inside the tent? And if they don't, if they can be in the tribe without exchanging money, that feels more like an expensive hobby to me than it does a business, broadly defined. All right. Let's see. Question, could you talk more about framework versus strategy? I think the best definition of framework is probably, what are the principles through which you're operating? You know, so, I mean, that's eight, I'm trying to think how I distinguish from strategy. So to go from sphere of influence is a framework. It's this idea that, and I don't have to paraphrase the whole thing, but there, you're going to, it's counterintuitive in that you're not trying to get the lead up front, which is frequently the idea. It's like, let me show you something and give you an ethical bribe so I can get you on my list. And then I will have my relationship with you via email over a period of time until I can convince you to buy, which may be in zero to 50 hours. I mean, who knows, depending on the business. There's also, there's kind of a broader launch model idea, which is, I wanna get you into this time bound sequence of events that's going to culminate with the reveal of an offer and a short period of time with the deadline. And we're gonna have this cohort of people who join. That's a framework, you know, product launch formula or launches in general are absolutely a framework to do business. Evergreen is a framework that, you know, you're saying what's not in, you know, certainly a product like Evergreen with deadline funnel. That's another way of, that's another framework because you have, the components are, yes, it's an Evergreen offer, but there's a discount that goes away once you've gone through a series of steps, the clock starts ticking. Okay, that's a framework. The way I have seen sphere of influence is at a principles level, it's lead with value and establish value and credibility first. Don't try to get the lead immediately, get the lead after and recognize that you're going to depress your lead conversion rates. But you're also going to look for, and in my experience, you're going to get a significant increase in conversion rates of the leads that you do get and there's a net positive there. So knowing that my strategy has to, has to be congruent with that. So my strategy is informed with a couple of principles. One, I'm not trying to get the lead immediately, I'm trying to provide value first. Two, my cost per lead is going to go up probably significantly. And three, what I'm looking for, as soon as I can figure it out, whether depending on my business model, is my value per lead significantly higher so that the total ROI makes more sense. In my experience, it does, that you get far fewer leads, far fewer. You get fewer leads and there's like a jamming lead gen campaign. However, the quality of those leads exponentially greater as measured in the short term and the long term. That's my experience. So that's the strategy and then the, or I'm sorry, that's the framework and then the strategy that informs that is, okay, well, I don't want to bring a lead gen strategy, making a promise in an ad to get this free PDF template, how to do XYZ and send them into my multi-page presale site where I'm not giving them XYZ, I'm leading through this long chain of beliefs. That strategy doesn't match. So my strategy, which is congruent with, certainly what Facebook wants in this example, is to reduce my lead, my customer click cost by giving Facebook exactly what it wants, which is good content and making that content engaging enough to get somebody all the way through that by the time they're at the end of that content, and if I have to assist them through retargeting, by the time they get to the end, they are so much higher quality when they do become a lead that I can afford to do that extra effort. So that's sort of the framework versus the strategy and then the tactics to do that are, video ad versus a long form copy versus short copy versus dynamic creative optimization with Facebook and on and on and on. Yeah, that's the point. You know, it's also, just because you're following a certain framework and you're building out your multi-page presale site, it doesn't mean it's gonna work well if you haven't followed those core principles that underlying strategy of the strategy of prevenants, you know, value first, giving a buck, those sorts of things, and then everything else gets laid out a couple of that. One big mistake I've seen, not from anyone specifically within the sphere of influence cohort, but where I've seen it out in the wild, is a multi-page presale site, that's a five-page sales letter with open loops. It's not supposed to be a sales letter. Like that's kind of like mistake number one and where it's like tricky copywriting that even if it doesn't feel salesy, it feels like kind of tricky, bullet pointy. It feels like sales copy. Like I feel like you're trying to convince me of something instead of revealing something to me, like push versus pull. Anytime I feel like I'm being pushed through a multi, what's something, but effectively would be a multi-page presale site, I wanna be pulled through it. I want my interest to, I want to feel like I am being drawn through it. And where I feel like I'm getting shoved along off in its often with a sales letter vibe. Yeah, it's like, I don't know, that's sort of like sphere of influence number one. You know, I hope there's like a billboard and the next iteration, it's like you're not writing a five-page sales letter, stop, hit the camera or something. All right, let's see here. So it's not a great answer to this question, but I do think you have to test the encapsulation of the idea as best you can without making it so curious somebody has to click. You can write, you can do really great narrative format ads that people have to click, or it's, you know, whatever, it was a dark and stormy night and such and such happened, and then this, and then all of a sudden, you're like, oh, all of a sudden, right? Because, you know, a lot of times you have to, you have to consider that your prospect is standing in line at the grocery store with his or her phone, and it's like, all right, then all of a sudden what? Like, you know, click, click, click. They're not in the mood to, you know, they're not gonna go through 10 pages of content. So don't trick them into giving you a false response. That's a long answer. Yeah, I'll, I'll, are you done? I just wanna add a little piece to the end. So what I would say is your page one of your multi-page doesn't need to be a thousand words or 2,000 words. It can be 500 or 400 words. I mean, you can start a story in 400 words, and Sean's totally right that it's that idea that you want to test first, right? So I would create two or three ideas that are each, say, 400 words each long. So it's long enough that people will either be interested or they won't be interested. And then there should be, in my opinion, it doesn't have to be, but in my opinion, I would track clicks onto the second page. And then that second page can be as simple as just saying there can be like an opt-in box, right? We're not quite finished with this thing. If you're interested in us letting you know once we finish writing this narrative, just put your pop-dynamic in my address inside here, and I'll let you know, that's it. But I wouldn't put that opt-in box on the front page because I want people to click over to the second page. So, you know, then you can just rotate those two or three page one versions, and then you can track to see which one of those three work well. And you might find that they're all working really well. One of the things I've done in the past is I've purposefully made one of the versions really bad. Like purposefully made it bad because I wanted to measure because, you know, if you put three versions out there and they all really good, and each one is resonating with a certain segment of that audience that you're getting over to that page, right? Well, it's difficult to find which one of those three or which one of those two is the idea that's resonating the best. So, I'll throw a dud inside there or something that's really bad, and then I'll rotate that with two or three others. And then you might find that your results say, yes, that very average bad one is the worst one because everyone's bouncing, but those other two or three actually are pretty close. So now that you know, well, actually, all those ideas are actually very good. So now you can, you know, you can then make decisions based on that. Well, people like all three ideas or two ideas, and then you can maybe have a choose your own adventure on that first page. You know, if you this, click here, if you this, click here, because you've already tested that those two ideas are working really well, and you would just get rid of the one that doesn't. And then you can now build out your entire narrative and then obviously email those people that did opt in on the second page during your, your testing phase. That's what I'll do. Yeah, that's brilliant. And I'll remind it of Glenn Livingston's comment. You know, we think we've tested, you know, we've tested 20 ads, but we've tested one ad 20 times. You know, so the way, what we, what we call it internally is AB plus Z testing. So to remind ourselves that, I mean, we're testing two variations of something very similar. The next thing we test needs to be wildly different, or we're just going to stay kind of confined to a little narrow part of the audience. So if you have two or three things that are thematically linked or two or three things that are of similar quality and intellectual approach, having one that's wildly different is important because then, then you're really getting, one is you're getting the micro view and the other you're getting a comparison at a macro view like, wow, okay. This one performed. And sometimes that's, you know, it depends on your audience, but it's very much language in the level at which the, the length, like the level at which the language is used. I mean, it's been stated way overstated a million times that, you know, Donald Trump tends to speak at about a fifth grade level. That's, you know, if we pull all the emotion or all the politics out of it and just look at that idea, and we talked about it and pretend it's not Donald Trump or something else, it's just, messaging one is written at the fifth grade level and messaging two is written at the postgraduate level. We're going to get a difference in response unless our audience is made entirely of, you know, postgraduate, you know, doctoral fellows or whatever. So it's something to test. It's just one of those things to be aware of. I love testing Facebook stuff. It's not, you know, we used to test a lot more with AdWords and then kind of testing stopped a lot with Facebook and Facebook does a lot of its own testing and allows for a lot of its own testing. And there's a lot that scratches the testing itch in Facebook when you're comparing audiences and ads and segments to sub-segments and you're like, wow, we're testing all day long, but I like idea testing too. So structure in writing, structure in thinking, structure in diagramming my thoughts, that's amazingly important to me. So I have to start with structure. So that and readability are two, and credibility are those are all factors. What I'm thinking about when I send somebody to something, doesn't have to be glitzy, doesn't have to be overly designed. Anyone who's seen my personal site knows design is not a big thing, but it does have to be readable. And the font size has to be something that somebody can read. It has to look like reasonably, it has to sound reasonably intelligent. I don't wanna send somebody to something and make my results not accurate because I haven't covered the basis with, is this going to, this is the question I ask myself, virtually every encounter that I have, and I've internalized it, how I approach agency work and advertising work, is contact with what I'm trying to make, somebody have contact with, so me, a client's material, whatever it is, is this going to make their day better by having been exposed to it? So this can be for me as simple as if I'm in the checkout line at a store and I have the opportunity to smile and be cordial to the person who is there working, is that going to make the difference in their day? No, but it's an opportunity to smile and be cordial and to, at a minimum, not make their day any worse, but hope in some capacity that that small interaction with me is a net positive for them. So then if somebody says, hey, what are you, I'm thinking about doing X, what do you know about X? To be able to go back and say, whatever example, I don't know a lot about that, but I do know somebody who does know a lot about that and I can make the connection and I've CC'd them on this email and here are some things I've read about it that I thought were interesting. Because objectively, I feel like I've made that situation better than neutral is my hope. So for advertising and for your specific example, the question I would ask myself is, these business owners, professionals in commercial real estate, after they've read my ad, page one, transition to page two, consume whatever content I'm showing, have I given them something that has made, their day better? Have I given them an insight? Have I framed something that seems like it's one way, but it can be another? Have I confirmed for them something that they already believe that my experience shows is true and they're on the right track? Have I pointed out to them where there's some quicksand that they may not be aware of that I've stumbled on or I know people who have and I can show them that, but have I given them value straight out? It just is all strategy preeminent stuff. That to me is the most important factor that if I looked at this person in the eyes across from me and I was sharing with them what I know, what I feel good about that interaction, like I've done my best on their behalf. Yes. And I'll imagine that, I'll jump in one extra piece is that, you need to be mindful of the fact that typically the more work you do on the front end, in mapping actual, your beliefs, ideally talking to or interviewing real human beings that are in your space that are trying to solve this problem. Not everybody does that, but it's gonna shortcut what comes later. So if you did a lot of guesswork on the front end and you came up with your channel beliefs based on your ideas or your kind of guess your way through it, it's gonna take a while to get to the point where that whole thing's gonna be working well and resonating. So it just depends on how long you wanna do the testing later on. It's worth, in my opinion, putting in the work on the front end, right up front and spending more time there because it's gonna save you more time later on. Nice. All right, next question from John. And this is where I think this is the meta, this is the meta use for sphere of influence that I like is probably my favorite part of it, maybe. I think it is my favorite part. So what I like to do is I sort of extrapolate, like, well, what's really going on with here? We're leading with value. We're not, we're, and we're leading with value when we're not making an ask of the person who's becoming aware of that value until we've given them a lot of value at a foundational level. So if I have a resort, for example, I have a resort client. So I might have, and this is actually true, we have this, we have a real-time board that shows the five most popular things that they're, and they have three primary audience segments for the resort site, for the leisure site. So we have the, there's three lanes, and we know in order which are most important for each of those segments. So we have an ad that goes to corresponding content on the site that is for each of those things. And when you look at them on real-time board, it looks exactly like a multi-page pre-sell site, except those pages aren't connected. We've made the connection, and we've made the connection in the order that those market segments actually care about those things. So the first thing that people care about in a resort setting, a quality resort setting, is what do the rooms look like? Research is clear on that. I wanna see the rooms. So do they care about spa? Absolutely. Do they care about all the stuff they can do? Absolutely. And they care about different things depending on if it's the age of the people, whether or not they have kids and other factors. So we have constructed a multi-page pre-sell site from content they already have that is not linked together in any way, except we've linked it together with their advertising campaign. That's the meta sphere. This is where you start packing on the layers of sphere of influence and realize, oh, okay, I thought it was this, but it's actually this, and then, oh, wait, it's not even that, it's this other thing. It's a principle, you know, that you can apply to literally everything. So I've seen a version that Range Rover, the four-wheel drive company, did many, well, a few years ago. I'm gonna try and find it, and then I'm gonna share it inside of the forum, because it was done really well. Now, Range Rover obviously didn't change their .com website, right, that stays the same. You don't mess with that, same with a big chain hotel, you're never gonna mess with their homepage, but you can create this external experience that brings in your pocket of people that you know are gonna be predisposed to travel, or lunging, or buying Range Rover. So Range Rover had created this long-form story narrative that was on a domain.com, it was Range Rover journey.com, for example. And it was long-form, but there was these beautiful, high-quality videos. So you would read a whole bunch of texts, get to a video, which then basically, people were jumping into a Range Rover, but the story itself wasn't about Range Rover, it was just this amazing narrative that people got sucked into this journey. And they were obviously targeting people that were into inventions, right? So, and these guys jumped into Range Rover, drove to a castle, and then the video stops, and you gotta click through it, because now they're gonna go through into the gate. And then it's page two, and then there's long-form copy again, then there's another video, and at every place, they're either wearing like a Range Rover logo somewhere, or there's a car, that's a Range Rover car, so it was very subtle, but it was clearly sponsored by Range Rover, for example, but the story itself wasn't about Range Rover per se. And that was a brilliant example of thinking outside of the box and them injecting their brand into that entire experience. And those people at the end of that were probably more predisposed to going to look at a Range Rover, buy a Range Rover, because they wanted to be part of that journey, and that experience had just been through. So, that's an example that can be applied to hotels, certainly restaurants, without missing about what their, dot-com, their main brand website, and you're building up a little sideline journey, that people, that's very shareable. People are gonna share these things on social media.
