You know, I want a Range Rover, Andre, perfect. Yeah. There actually, an example came to mind, see when we were talking that, I have a client who has really, really good video content, and we made, I'm gonna be careful, I say this, because I've never explicitly said this with this client, that we actually made a sphere of influence approach, but that's exactly what I did, is I externalized the needs that, I sort of had a reverse engineer, the needs that they were expressing through the positives that they were showing. You know, our product does X, and what's the need associated with X? Daisy Chain those, they're a very long webinar that I could extract out the, I think we did two to three minute, maybe one five minute segment, we can extract out the really compelling sections in Daisy Chain those across the beliefs, and that at any point in time, when somebody has engaged with any of those at any point, they can shortcut the whole process and see the full 45 minutes. So, if you were to look at them individually, you're like, well, this is, you're just using video content. Right, however, if you step back at 30,000 feet and walk down it, how it's all linked together, it's a video based sphere of influence, multi-page pre-sell journey is really what it is. And that's exactly the structure that I use when I did it. So, don't be limited to the site idea as much as the beliefs as individual entities idea, or the content ideas, and don't make the mistake of saying, well, I have a bunch of great blog content, I'll just put my top 10 articles together and call it sphere of influence. It's not what we're talking about. It is doing the work to figure out the beliefs, figuring out or in identifying where you've addressed those beliefs in your existing content, and then putting all of that together with structure and taking somebody through that journey so that when they reach the end of that journey, they are far more likely to become, to go from prospect to customer. That's really the goal. All right, we've got 16 minutes here. So, the question is, do the strategic principles you outline in sphere of influence for Facebook apply to Google, display network, or is it completely different animal? If they are similar, are there any important differences we should be mindful of? I, unless you're, this is, this may be the more controversial thing that I say today, but if that's true, that's probably a good thing. But if you can avoid it, don't do the Google display network unless you're an expert. And I don't say that lightly. I love the GDN. I would use it for retargeting. That's what I mentioned in the paid traffic training. I would absolutely use it for retargeting across and have it match up with your retargeting efforts that are behaviorally based. The reason I say, I mean, you can do managed placements. You can do, there's, there are a lot of really good strategies for the GDN. Problem is there are two fundamental problems. One is banner blindness. We've just, we've gotten really good at ignoring ads when they appear up here everywhere. So it's a, it is a high volume, to do the GDN right is high volume, generally high cost, because you have to test so many different variables and often test them simultaneously. There are easier, lower cost, lighter entry points in the GDN, but they're so highly specialized that I feel like we would be ignoring the real value. Facebook has given us, and I love, I'm an AdWords guy, I haven't been forever. I don't, I'm not jumping on the Facebook bandwagon because I, you know, I'm a Facebook fanboy. I just, what Facebook does relative to what the GDN does, it's so much different. And we have so much more information on Facebook that I would start with Facebook and I would hold off on the GDN as a front end strategy and leave it as a retargeting strategy. It's just, there's too many ways to burn cash and not just burn a little cash. You can burn a lot of cash on the GDN and not get a great result. And I just don't want anybody to do that. Let's see here. I'm just gonna keep going in order, I think. What is the strategy to test ads? For example, some can, the same campaign and multiple ad sets or keep the ad set and keep duplicating ads within it. I don't wanna, I don't wanna go too tactical on this just because a very basic strategy for, we kind of covered the A, B testing and stuff earlier, so we may have answered this already. At first when testing, I like to know absolutely what I'm testing and I wanna have a hypothesis and I wanna either dis, I wanna prove it, disprove it or not be able to do either and then know that as fast as possible. So if I think I have a great idea for a hook and an entry point into a multi-page pre-sell site and I test one expression of that versus the other and they both do equally well, I just know that they both did equally well and I don't really know much. So I have this piece of information and I have to figure out, well, all right, those both drill, what's just for the sake of argument, say it's high quality content so you've got an inexpensive click cost. If I look at that and say, is this good enough? It's a sort of idea from Barry Schwartz and the paradox of choice that instead of being a maximizer, you'd be a satisficer. It's already made up where you're sort of satisfied with the result that sort of minimum result that matters. So if I'm testing an ad and I just wanna know will this audience respond and go read content and I get a response and it's inexpensive, the maximizer is like, well, let's do 50 variations and let's see which one is the best that we could spend and all of a sudden you spent a week doing something that is just making, it's infinitely more complex and added work and other things but you already got a result that was satisfactory. So I would suggest in that example is don't get too wound about testing at that point. If the result is okay enough to keep going, then keep going and then you go from okay, this ad gets attention, gets a click, perfect. Now does that audience and that ad get somebody to go from page one to page two? That's all I care about and once I get a result for page one to page two, and let's just say for the sake of argument that I've told myself that my minimum needs to be 20%. Okay, and I get 5%, I'm not doing anything until I fix that problem. If I get 20%, I'm not trying to get 40%. Okay, I said 20, I got 20, let me move down to opt-in rate. Because opt-in rate's the next thing that I care about. So now I have an okay result from ad to page one, I have an acceptable rate from page one to page two. Now what's my opt-in rate? If my target's 10% and I get 10%, now I wanna see what the take rate is for the eventual offer. Where I see testing go horribly wrong is when testing becomes the outcome and testing is supposed to be in service to the outcome. And if we try to maximize, then testing becomes a thing unto itself. And I play guilty here with that, I love that stuff. But ultimately what we care about is the end goal of the system. We can come back and look for the system optimum later. But initially for traction, I wanna test just enough to get an acceptable result. And that's it. And Andrea, you may have some different thoughts on that one. You're muted, Andrea. Nope, just mindful of the fact that we've, I've been wittling through some of the questions to try and keep the ones that are most relevant to do with the paid traffic. Okay, I'll jump on the next one then. Question for both of us. I'm going to, that's the thing I'm actually going to do. So my answer to that, I think will be my own project because I'm not doing it for intellectuals, well, I am sort of doing it for an intellectual stimulation, but I'm doing it because I expect an economic result that's ROI, significantly ROI positive. And I'm going to show everybody how I would get from here to there. And one thing I would suggest not doing is starting an agency. I don't expect agencies will be a viable business model and sort of say publicly now, three to five years. There will be a consulting model, there still will be agencies, but the pace at which the tools available for optimization within the platforms are so great that human beings are not going to be able to keep up on the strategy side and Facebook is building those tools as fast as possible. And if we can project out and see the writing on the wall and see the acceleration of the pace of change, highly informed consultants will be valuable, I believe in three to five years, but the mechanics of doing the work and testing and getting results, Facebook is far better at that. So to answer your question specifically, does the cycle get longer? It depends on how much of a burning need there is in your audience and how much you have really honed in on the beliefs that are required. So I think that's a great question. You have really honed in on the beliefs that are required. I mean, I think if there are two things that I think are true to make this work, to shorten the cycle as much as possible, really getting the needs right and being the first person who has ever told your prospect their needs and language that they can understand. You're the first person who's really said to them, I get you and I know exactly what you mean and you articulate it to them in your multi-page pre-sell site in a way that no one has ever explained it to them before. And you show them that it's not their fault and that it's really the environment in which or the methods that they've tried. That's the problem, they're not the problem and that you've taken people who are just like them to their desired future. I think that's very potent. I do think Rich Sheffrin, he's masterful in his, there's a video in Sphere of Influence where he, which is probably among the best videos I've ever seen. I've watched it more times and I care to count. And he talks about this idea that when you can make the way people are currently solving the problem, the actual problem and that what they think is the problem are symptoms of the problem. And the way he explains this, and I love it, is he said, we think the problem is X, so we do Y, but the problem is actually Z and Y makes Z worse. If you die, I had to diagram that out like 12 times to figure it out, but I think in your market, Carol, which I know a little bit about, I think what you need to position yourself to really shorten your cycle, to really be able to take somebody from exposure to you and your ideas through to becoming a customer, I think you need to really show them with clarity that the way they've been trying to solve the problems that they have is actually the problem and that what you are offering is the thing that makes those problems go away because you're not doing what everybody else is doing, you're addressing the real problem and what they've been doing has been making the problem worse. This is not Carolyn's market, so I will make this up a little bit, but if you sell an online course to, I don't know, get typical digital marketing course and in everybody's selling, how to make your first bazillion dollar funnel. And if there are 1,000 people selling the how to make your first bazillion dollar funnel course and someone has gone through 20 of them, 30 of them, 40 of them, and you're the person who stands up and says, what you really wanna do is have a stable predictable income and because of that, you're going through course after course after course, which you think is solving the problem and giving you the tools, but what it's actually doing is it's distracting you from doing the three or four things you really need to do consistently to get results that are not found in the programs that you're doing. So the thing you think you're doing to solve the problem is the problem. So stop doing that and do this other thing instead. That is that that's as powerful as it gets. And Rich is the first person I know of who has really articulated that structurally in a way that can be repeated. So for your, the short answer to your question is, conventionally, Carolyn, yes, you could really extend the time it takes to make a customer using this approach, comma, but I don't think it has to be that way. I think you could actually speed up the cycle, because you have all the ingredients, the authority, the credibility, the history and all these things. So you have all of those things already in place. And now you just need to bring somebody through that really compelling journey to say, I know who you are. I know the struggles you've faced. I know how you've tried to solve it in the past. And guess what? It wasn't your fault, it was the methods you tried. And if in order to get the result that you want, you have to stop trying those methods and do this instead, instead, that's where I think the goal is for you. Yeah, I just want to add something else that, let's say you've got that funnel working, good enough or it's kind of working. This isn't about stopping that and doing, for example, which is for you, it's untested and you don't know what that cycle length is going to be. You must totally do what I'm sure I just said first because that's going to shorten it anyway. And then once you've got it working and you've tested this thing out and you haven't spent tons of money, then you can start to taper off on your main ad spend to the point where maybe you can completely replace the current offer with one of the final. Yeah, one last thing to add to that too, to minimum for everybody, Carol's a good example, but to minimize cost and really get a sense like is this going to work, there's, you don't have to have a thousand people, I mean, depending on your product, but you get the targeting rate and you get other things right and you instead of spending money for three months, four months, five months, it's absolutely okay to get a hundred, say just do a hundred leads. We get a hundred people in it a condensed period of time through this particular process and then actually engage with those hundred personally by email, ask questions, like research, research, research, turn traffic off at that point, get the result, just back and forth with those people, and maybe 200, whatever it is for your business, 100, 200, whatever, you know how much it costs to get them, then you're engaging with them and engaging with them, asking them questions like a fanatic and over delivering and over delivering. And at some point you do make an offer to that audience and maybe your cycle is such that you're making, you're starting, you're starting front end cold traffic and you're making an offer in 60 days or 45 days or whatever when you're doing your time-based programs. So what you just care about is, yes they're getting some extra care and attention but that's a great model anyway, but when you paid, let's just say for the sake of argument, it costs $500, you paid $500 and you got a hundred prospects in and you engage with them and you got some information from them and when it's time and you've built a relationship and when it's time to convert, do five of them convert or do none of them convert? If none of them convert, then we're really glad that you didn't spend the other 40 days or whatever it was churning through $500 a week and ad spend to get a thousand people to realize that that cycle is just, it's not long enough or whatever it is. I mean, is this perfectly accurate? No, but it's directly accurate. Cause what I see happen a lot is it's like, okay, for the next 90 days, we're gonna jam, jam, jam on Facebook and we're not sure if Facebook traffic will convert and we get all the way to day 90 and then to tally up what happened, it was like, wow, I spent $10,000 and I didn't get a customer in those 90 days from Facebook. Facebook's worthless. Like, well, that's not actually what the data showed. So what I would rather do is have a smaller amount to get however much of a cohort you need to feel like it's enough people to engage with that you can spend the minimum amount of money and you get sort of the first one doesn't have the big impact, but it also, this goes back to mistake avoidance. You don't drop 10 grand in that, spend your five grand, whatever it is. You spend a tenth of that or whatever it is, 5% of that and you get some result and maybe you find out of those pundit in that cohort that when you do go to make the offer, either they don't have the money or they didn't resonate with them or they can't commit the time they don't really believe it's right for them. Perfect, you have an answer. You know how the next time you get your cohort of 100 that you have to address some of these questions early and then you get another result. You don't get the big bang impact as fast because you don't have the 500, 600,000, 5000 leads in the system, but you also don't spend all the cash it takes to get them to find out that that cash didn't turn in the customers. So that's mistake avoidance at its best. So I think if I woke up thinking out loud, if I woke up tomorrow knowing what I know owning your business, I would do a cohort test of I want 200. The paradox of choice, by the way, is it's a 10 video, it's Barry Schwarz. He wrote a book, don't read the book, just watch the video, it's the same thing. John asks, I have 4,700 word e-book. I ran traffic team with Facebook ads with the goal of conversions. My lander converted at 50%. I assume that's opt-in conversion 50%. I'm considering turning this into my presale site then moving them right into the sphere, my SOS sequence, soap opera sequence. Any specifics I should look for to compare results? Just measure on the second page visitors or measure actual lead gen. I wouldn't do apples to apples lead gen because it's just so fundamentally different to have somebody hit something in opt-in for a lead because they really want your content. So you can't really compare somebody who really wants the content and they get the content as a lead to someone who gets the access to all the content and then chooses to jump in after that relationship with you. It's just not an accurate comparison. So what you've described, I would absolutely do. I would take the content from that e-book, if it makes sense, to do it that way or restructure it in a way that you're opening and closing loops based on beliefs from page to page. I would use that content to make the multi-page pre-sale site and then I would calculate the cost to acquire a lead based on that strategy versus the cost to acquire a lead with a traditional strategy. And right after I saw those two numbers side by side, I would forget that I ever did that comparison because it's not meaningful, but everybody wants to do it, so I would do it. And what I would really care about is the value per visitor. Our conversion cost per conversion for a customer is what I would really care about. So if you send a thousand people to an opt-in page to get your e-book, and then they have an opportunity to become a customer, for every thousand dollars you spent doing that, how many dollars did you make? And then I would compare that number apples to apples against doing that whole process where you're putting the content out first, getting the lead later. If I were a betting person, I would bet that the second version is going to cost significantly more to get the lead and have so much better value per lead that you're not going to care about lead costs at that point. And you're just going to see those two numbers don't, you can't compare them side by side. What really matters is, and this is really the fundamental idea behind, I think, running paid traffic for sphere of influence is that the net gain you get from putting up the red velvet rope and making people go through more hurdles and demonstrating your authority and your credibility and your knowledge and subject matter expertise and framing the world for somebody like all of that, the net gain so far outperforms the additional cost that it's laughable. So the cost isn't really a cost. That's my opinion and my experience as well. All right, art based on what you are seeing with Facebook, what template seems to be working best right now. So I wanna be cautious about that. Last question. Yeah, sorry, here we go. Last question. We can do this forever. I love this stuff. So Steven asks, how long does it take you on average to think through and strategize a paid traffic campaign? What's your process? Us is a fun question. My process is messy. It's non-linear and it's weird as hell. So full disclosure, we talk about being honest here. So I'm gonna tell you exactly what I do and recognize a couple of things that are very important when you understand what I do versus how you do what I do. I've done this for a long time. Probably managed somewhere north of $50 million in ad spend. I have 450 plus design build projects just on and on and on with client relationships. So there's so much outside my conscious awareness that what feels a lot to me like magic most likely is a little magic and magic meaning that we don't really understand how it works, but also it's just a lot of experience. So if I describe my process and you do what I describe and you don't get the same result, it's nothing wrong with you at all. It's just, this is my own weirdness. You'll find your way in here. So here's my understanding of the creative process and my experience of the creative process, which is there is a struggle phase and this is borrowing that lots of people have done this work. This is not my work. So there is a struggle phase or an accumulation phase or a data gathering phase. I call this in my own vernacular collection. So if I'm researching something or if I'm learning about a client in your business, my documents are appended with a dash collection, my real-time boards or other tools that I use are appended with this dash collection because that's just me gathering whatever I think I need to know about how does the client make money? What have they said? What have they tried? What insights can I get? What kind of feedback do they have? What's common in their testimonials? I mean, do you name it? I am an eight on the Colby FactFinder scale. So I have to do this for a long time to feel like I really understand it. And at some point I feel like I've done that and this is not, there's no hard and fast rule, but at some point I feel like I've done that and I've accumulated it. And then all of a sudden very quickly I get very tired and that's my signal that I'm done with a struggle phase, the accumulation and the collection phase. I like that phase even though it's called struggle, whatever I enjoy it, I find flow in that, I can hours disappear. I don't ever set time with that. When I estimate with clients, I generally don't even estimate that time, I enjoy it. You can't pay me to do that because it wouldn't make sense what you would have to pay for the amount of time I spend in that, but that's just for me to be a good representative. So this is true for me when I learn stuff too, it's the same process. After that collection phase and that initial phase, there's a phase called release. J.M.G. Young talked about this in the excellent book, The Technique for Producing Ideas. It's a foundation of creative theory that at some point you have to deliberately stop trying to either solve a problem or come up with a solution or imagine whatever you have to let it go. And when I say let it go, I let it go. And I tell people this very openly. Oftentimes a client will say, okay, well, I know you're working on this one day and Tuesday, can we set up a call Wednesday so we can go over what you came up with? And my answer is always no. It is, I'm going to do this work. And then it's, and I'm gonna let it go. And at some point things will reveal themselves to me. And then when that happens, then I get back to work. For me, that's 24 to 48 hours, just like a clock. I accumulate, accumulate, accumulate. I stop trying to do that. I don't try to make sense of anything. I know my subconscious is turning on it. Often my sleep gets a little wonky if I do this too late at night. But I'm not actively trying to do anything at that point. And often I'll go, I don't go do that same phase with another client right after that introduces, then I get a weird like this plus this and I get a solution. It doesn't make any sense. So I don't actively look for other inputs at that point and for in a different topic because it messes me up. So 24 to 48 hours goes by. I feel it physiologically. Again, full disclosure of weirdness, I get it. I won't go, actually I'll just tell you how I feel, I feel like my fingertips very weird. Like I start getting a sense and an anxiousness to go do some work related to it. And once that starts to happen, I know the muse is about to show up. And 24 to 48 hours later, the muse shows up and I'm starting to get this feeling shows up. And depending on how I do it, at that point it feels like transcription. I don't feel like it's as sort of creative, like I'm actively doing it. I feel like I'm just transcribing something that has appeared. I see the structure very clearly. I see the inner relationships very clearly. And then I have an enormous whiteboard behind me. I have whiteboards all over my office. I'm a visual spatial thinker. So I think both visually and in space so that I walk a lot and I walk back and forth and things have meaning for me in different locations. So I will like at the other end of my office about 30 feet from here, there's a whiteboard that's dedicated to the value proposition design canvas. So that's where all that happens. And then the pacing between there and my other office is where the thinking happens to extrapolate this. So I go through that process and then I draw and I wipe boards. Sometimes I write a narrative. But at that point it's pretty much fully formed in my mind which I believe is the byproduct of experience and pouring in data into that experience structure. And then I very rarely do, can I stop? Once this starts, it does feel like transcription. I tend to go beginning to end and I see the whole thing in front of me and I transcribe it and I digitize it. And I will often narrate whatever a client needs to know or whatever at that point. I do not very rarely have I been successful digitizing first even though I see it. I have to analog make it real and then I can digitize it. So that's my process. It is very unique to me, very odd. The how long does it take is the biggest factor is how much do I need to research to feel comfortable? And sometimes I have to research an entire industry. Sometimes I have to research just a client's representation in industry. Sometimes I really, really, really like the material. So my research is actually more of a small project where I just have to go deep and then pop up later like wow, it just happened, it's been a week. But I would say in general this part of the process takes seven to 21 days depending on if the magic happens or not. Long answer to a short question. It's a very strange process, but it's so finely tuned to who I am that I find great comfort in that process and great, great joy in that. And that's the same process I used to learn anything. I love to learn stuff. So I apply that process to virtually everything. The only thing that gets a little difficult for that process is when it's a physical skill. Life long martial artists have to learn a physical skill is different because you have to have that practice and bumping up against feeling completely stupid and pushing that competence level. If you do the same thing that if you do something that you already know how to do and call it practice, it's not really practice. Practice is doing something just outside your ability to do it well. So that's a great question to close on. Andra, anything to jump in here with? You have another question. That was an awesome response, I think. And I'm also a crazy learner, but I think just to put in the time and not to rush it, you'll know when you're ready, and then you move forward again. Somebody just had a great comment in the chat. It says, I follow the same creative process. Glad to hear that I'm not alone and not crazy. All we know is that you're not alone. You can't confirm that you're not crazy. We're all crazy.
